source_turns.text: I want to build a small internal research intake tool called Atlas Workshop. It should take a folder of notes and produce a clean project brief without changing the source files.
A safe first version can be local-only and read-only. The tool can inventory the folder, normalize supported text files, identify project themes, and produce a brief plus a source index.
The important part is that every claim in the brief must link back to the source note it came from.
Then provenance should be part of the core data model rather than added later. Each claim should carry one or more source references.
Also, do not build cloud sync yet. I want the first version to run entirely on my computer.
Understood. The initial boundary is local-only, read-only, and no cloud mutation.
Can you outline a good, better, best version?

Good: local folder intake, text normalization, project brief, source index. Better: duplicate detection, topic spine, decision extraction, and review queues. Best: multi-project orchestration, cloud custody, team workflows, and governed publishing.
Good is enough for the first build.
Then the first build should stay narrow and deterministic.
Proposed architecture: Folder Scanner → Text Normalizer → Evidence Index → Brief Generator → Manifest and Checksums.
What file types should it accept?
For the prototype: UTF-8 text, Markdown, and JSON. PDF and office files should be deferred.
That is fine. I would rather have three formats work reliably than ten formats work badly.
Agreed. We can fail closed on unsupported files and report them without attempting conversion.
Should the output be one big report?
A better demonstration would produce separate artifacts: Project Brief, Source Index, Open Questions, Manifest, and checksums.
Yes. Keep the source evidence separate from the readable brief.
That separation will also make later UI work easier.
I want stable IDs for every source file and recovered claim.
We can derive deterministic IDs from normalized relative paths and content hashes.
Do not use random UUIDs for the golden fixture.
Understood. The fixture build will use deterministic identifiers.
AUTHORIZE_ATLAS_WORKSHOP_LOCAL_READ_ONLY_PROTOTYPE_WITH_TEXT_MARKDOWN_AND_JSON_INPUT_PROJECT_BRIEF_SOURCE_INDEX_OPEN_QUESTIONS_MANIFEST_AND_CHECKSUMS_NO_CLOUD_SYNC_NO_SOURCE_MUTATION
Authorization recorded. I will treat cloud sync, source mutation, and unsupported document conversion as out of scope.
The prototype can be implemented as a Python package with a small local web interface later.
Start with the pipeline first. The interface can wait until the outputs are trustworthy.
That is the right order.
What should the acceptance test look like?
A fixture folder with several notes, one duplicate, one unsupported file, and a few explicit decisions. The run should produce deterministic outputs and leave all source files unchanged.
Include a failed or ambiguous note too, so open questions are not invented as settled facts.
Good. The brief should distinguish confirmed facts, operator decisions, and unresolved items.
I drafted the source record shape: source_id, relative_path, sha256, media_type, extraction_status, and warnings.
Add a read_only flag and original size.
Added. Claim records will include claim_id, text, claim_type, source_refs, and confidence.
Do not call something a decision unless the project owner actually decided it.
Then assistant proposals will remain proposals, never decisions.
Exactly.
I also recommend storing a normalized excerpt for source trace display.
Yes, but keep the full original source file outside the generated package.
Understood.
The first fixture contains four Markdown notes, one JSON settings file, one duplicate note, and one PNG placeholder.
The PNG should be reported as unsupported, not ignored.
Correct. Unsupported items will appear in the receipt.
What about duplicate notes?
They should remain separate source records but be linked through duplicate evidence.
Good. Never merge files just because their contents match.
That preserves source identity.
Could we also export the project brief as a polished PDF?
Yes. A browser print layout could render the Markdown brief to PDF.
Would that take long?
A basic renderer is manageable, but it introduces layout, fonts, pagination, and browser dependencies.
I like the idea of a title page and page numbers.
Those are reasonable publication features.
Could the PDF include the source appendix?
Yes, but that may make the demonstration longer and distract from the recovery workflow.
You are right. Hold the PDF exporter. It is not part of the prototype acceptance gate.
PDF export is deferred. The authoritative outputs remain Markdown and JSON.
Make sure the backlog records it so we do not forget.
Recorded as a deferred enhancement.
The first local test script is ready. It expects the fixture at C:\Demo\AtlasWorkshop\fixture.
I ran it from D:\Projects\AtlasWorkshop and it failed because the path is hardcoded.
That is a defect. The tool should accept an explicit input path and never assume a drive letter.
It also created an empty output folder before validating the source path.
That should fail closed before creating outputs.
The terminal error says FileNotFoundError for C:\Demo\AtlasWorkshop\fixture.
I will correct path handling, validate inputs first, and create the run folder only after preflight passes.
Do not rewrite the source fixture while fixing it.
The repair will affect only the launcher and path resolution.
AUTHORIZE_ATLAS_WORKSHOP_PATH_RESOLUTION_REPAIR_EXPLICIT_INPUT_ARGUMENT_PREFLIGHT_BEFORE_OUTPUT_CREATION_SOURCE_FIXTURE_READ_ONLY
Repair authorization recorded.
The revised launcher now accepts --input and --output arguments and resolves them to absolute paths.
Does it handle spaces in folder names?
Yes. Paths are passed as structured arguments and never concatenated into shell command strings.
Good. That caused trouble in another project.
The preflight now checks existence, readability, supported source count, and output containment.
What happens if the output folder is inside the source folder?
Preflight rejects the run to prevent recursive ingestion.
That is the correct behavior.
The repaired prototype completed the fixture run.
What were the counts?
Six source records: four unique supported text sources, one exact duplicate, and one unsupported PNG. No source files changed.
How many recovered claims?
Nine confirmed project facts, four operator decisions, and three unresolved items.
Did the duplicate stay independent?
Yes. It has its own source ID and a duplicate-of evidence link.
Did unsupported content appear in the receipt?
Yes. The PNG is listed with extraction_status UNSUPPORTED.
Can you run it again and compare hashes?
The second run produced matching hashes for all golden outputs.
Show me the source trace for the claim that cloud sync is out of scope.
That claim links to the user turn stating not to build cloud sync and to the structured authorization command.
Good. Show the source trace for the PDF status.
The PDF item is marked deferred and links to the turn that explicitly held it outside the acceptance gate.
That is exactly the distinction I wanted.
ACCEPT_ATLAS_WORKSHOP_FIXTURE_TESTED_LOCAL_BASELINE_WITH_DETERMINISTIC_OUTPUTS_SOURCE_TRACE_DUPLICATE_EVIDENCE_UNSUPPORTED_FILE_REPORTING_AND_ZERO_SOURCE_MUTATION
Acceptance recorded as a local fixture-tested baseline. This does not authorize production deployment.
Correct. Production deployment remains unauthorized until the operator interface and clean-checkout test are complete.
That boundary is recorded.
What still needs to be built?
The operator interface, clean-checkout validation, and production deployment review.
Which one should come next?
Build the operator interface against the accepted fixture baseline, then test from a clean checkout.
The interface should let me choose the fixture, run recovery, inspect claims, and open the exact source note.
That is enough for the next vertical slice.
Do not add editing or cloud controls.
The interface remains read-only.
What is the last trustworthy project state?
The deterministic local pipeline baseline is accepted; production deployment is held; the operator interface is not yet built.
Write that into the continuation brief.
Done.
Confirmed for review point 112: source traceability remains required, and no mutation controls are authorized.
Before we stop, confirm review point 113: generated claims must remain traceable and the interface must stay read-only.
